title: Preparing Biolog Growth Plates
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"bo039","text":"Overview and Goals","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c950","text":"Your bacterial isolate has been grown on agar plates. You practiced pipetting. Now, let’s learn about how your organism grows! What nutrients help your bacterium grow? We will use plates with ninety-six wells with different conditions to examine the growth and metabolic capacity of your isolate. For this, we will use special Biolog plates and your Blood Universal Growth (BUG) media containing plates with your organism. Biolog plates allow researchers to test and visually (colorimetrically!) analyze the growth of bacteria in numerous conditions… up to 30 conditions in one plate! Biolog plates work by having powder chemicals in the wells along with an inoculating fluid and a dye that changes color when an organism is growing (“metabolic dye”). materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"2108f","text":"Activity 1:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c98fp","text":"One 200 μl multichannel micropipette (p200)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4m609","text":"Micropipette tips for p200","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"68puq","text":"One 96-well plate","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d1edn","text":"Kimwipes","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4ml9","text":"One container with water with food coloring (yellow)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1cqln","text":"One container with water with food coloring (green)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1je92","text":"Tip disposal container","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cqmk4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3n8tv","text":"Activities 2-3:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ej210","text":"One Inoculatorz™ swab","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2d6rp","text":"IF-A inoculating fluid","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"128a4","text":"Tryptic Soy Agar (TSA) plate with bacteria","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3ri3m","text":"Biolog GEN3 plate","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"81cob","text":"One 200 μl multichannel micropipette (p200)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2n209","text":"Micropipette tips for p200","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"397db","text":"Kimwipes","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dr8pb","text":"Tip disposal container","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Set your p200 multichannel pipet to 100 µL
Load eight (8) tips onto each gasket of the multichannel pipet.
Press the plunger TO the first stop.
Submerge all 8 tips into the water with food coloring.
With thumb control, release the plunger to take up liquid. Check each tip to make sure each has the same volume of liquid. Note the height of the liquid in the tips. Is it consistent?
Align each tip with one well across one column of the 96-well plate (refer to the image or the descriptions below)
Yellow water should go in odd numbered columns 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11.
Green water should go in the even numbered columns 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12.

If using colors other than yellow and green, make your own pattern. Reference image: Each researcher will use multi-channel pipette to transfer 100uL of colored solutions to each numbered columns of the 96-well plate. Check that you are delivering the appropriate volume to all wells.
Press the plunger THROUGH the first stop, raise your tips up out of the plate, and then you may release your plunger with thumb control.
Have each researcher do two to three columns of the plate to practice.

We will swab bacterial cells from your agar plates (BUG Agar: BUG (Biolog Universal Growth) Agar w/ 5% sheep blood), dilute in Biolog IF-A inoculation fluid, and check turbidity with a turbidimeter. Calibrate the turbidimeter to a "blank" sample of inoculation fluid in a tube

Using the Biolog Turbidimeter, blank the turbidimeter with a clean tube containing uninoculated IF-A. Wipe the tube clean of dirt and fingerprints Because the tubes used are not optically uniform, they should be blanked individually.
Set the 100% transmittance adjustment knob so that the meter reads 100%.
Record reading.
Use a turbidimeter with bacterial sample in the inoculation fluid in a tube
Collect bacteria from your streak plate by touching the Biolog Inoculatorz™ swab to the bacteria Be gentle: do not push the swab through the agar media

Open your Biolog Inoculating Fluid tube (IF) tube and submerge the swab into the liquid. Swirl the swab around in the fluid for 00:00:15 to ensure inoculation. Do not leave the liquid uncapped longer than necessary to prevent contamination. The tubes are glass: work carefully.
Cap the tube and gently invert the three (3) times to ensure thorough mixing.

The target cell density is 95%T for our protocol. Add more bacteria with the swab if necessary to get as close to this value as possible by continuing to add more bacteria with the swab to the tube. Work carefully and keep the tube capped. If necessary, use another swab if you think it has touched another surface.
Once you've reached the target cell density at 95%T , cap the tube and proceed to the next Activity.

Each researcher will use a multi-channel pipette to transfer 100uL of inoculating fluid and bacteria to each numbered column of the 96-well plate. Check that you are delivering the appropriate volume to all wells. Pour the cell suspension into the multichannel pipette reservoir.
Load 8 tips onto each gasket of the multichannel pipet and fill the tips by drawing up 100 µL of cell suspension from the reservoir.

Use the multichannel to add 100 µL of diluted bacterial culture to each of the 96 wells of the GEN III MicroPlate TM . Use the technique practiced in Activity 1. If the tips touch the wells, replace your tips before delivering inoculation fluid to the next column. Work carefully to avoid contamination!
Eject the pipettor tips if any one tip touches the wells of the plate as this can cross contamination wells with chemicals. Reload tips as needed.
Check that all wells have liquid
Cover the GEN3 microplate with its lid and eject the pipette tips.

Incubate at 33 °C for 24:00:00 - 24:00:00, 2d hours. For some plates, we will incubate in a heated Agilent/Biotek LogPhase 600 plate reader and take 590 nm/600 nm readings every 15 minutes for 2 days to learn about the growth patterns. Reference image: Illustration of Biolog GEN3 plate with several purple wells. Right: icon of calendar with text "Incubate the plate at 33'C for 1 to 2 days" Clean Up Instructions Biolog growth plates must be given to an instructor to place in our plate readers to measure viability and growth! All liquids from Activity 1 (materials with no bacterial contamination) can be poured down the sink and plastic can go in the regular trash. All liquids containing bacteria must be poured into a beaker for bleach decontamination (can be found on the blue bench paper by this sink labeled "liquid"). Glass tubes can be placed into a beaker (can be found on the blue bench paper by this sink labeled "glass"). All materials from Activity 2 that contact bacteria need to go into the biohazard trash. All bacterial growth plates can be thrown away in the biohazard trash Return all reusable materials to our storage drawers. Wipe down all surfaces with Conflikt and then ethanol. Remove PPE. Wash hands. Critical Thinking Questions for Preparing Biolog Growth Plates What are some advantages of using a multichannel pipette? What are some disadvantages of using a multichannel pipette? During the inoculation, describe how you ensure that you isolate a "pure culture." Why is a "pure culture" needed for this assay? What is a turbidimeter, and why would measuring turbidity be important for this assay?
creator: Nidhi Grover
